coredata.dc:description: Multi-functional film materials that enable monitoring and control of food safety are effective tools for addressing food safety issues. In this study, nitrogen-doped citric acid carbon dot (NCD) functionalized with surface potassium ions was incorporated into chitosan film to confer film pH-monitoring capability. The inclusion complex (HPCDGer) of hydroxypropyl cyclodextrin (HPCD) and geraniol (Ger) was prepared and added as antimicrobial material to the chitosan film. With the increase in pH, the fluorescence intensity of the composite film increased significantly. CS/NCD@HPCDGer exhibited excellent UV shielding, water vapor barrier, antioxidant (Scavenging activity of DPPH and ABTS were 74.01% and 92.48%, respectively), and antimicrobial effects. The inhibition rates against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were 99.82% and 95.89%, respectively. The pH, weight loss, and total volatile alkaline nitrogen (TVB-N) of the pork packed in the composite film were significantly lower. Furthermore, the composite film is capable of showing orange fluorescence in response to biogenic amines from pork spoilage. These results indicate that the composite film has potential applications in the monitoring and control of food safety.

head.ce:title: Chitosan film loaded with carbon dots and geraniol/hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin inclusion complex: Simultaneous freshness preservation and quality monitoring of pork

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-functional film materials that enable monitoring and control of food safety are effective tools for addressing food safety issues. In this study, nitrogen-doped citric acid carbon dot (NCD) functionalized with surface potassium ions was incorporated into chitosan film to confer film pH-monitoring capability. The inclusion complex (HPCDGer) of hydroxypropyl cyclodextrin (HPCD) and geraniol (Ger) was prepared and added as antimicrobial material to the chitosan film. With the increase in pH, the fluorescence intensity of the composite film increased significantly. CS/NCD@HPCDGer exhibited excellent UV shielding, water vapor barrier, antioxidant (Scavenging activity of DPPH and ABTS were 74.01% and 92.48%, respectively), and antimicrobial effects. The inhibition rates against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were 99.82% and 95.89%, respectively. The pH, weight loss, and total volatile alkaline nitrogen (TVB-N) of the pork packed in the composite film were significantly lower. Furthermore, the composite film is capable of showing orange fluorescence in response to biogenic amines from pork spoilage. These results indicate that the composite film has potential applications in the monitoring and control of food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The waste of resources caused by food spoilage and the harm caused by the consumption of spoiled food are significant problems in the world today (Karanth et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2021). According to statistics, nearly 1.3 billion tonnes of food are lost to spoilage each year in the world, which is approximately one-third of total production (Kumar & Sarkar, 2018). In addition, foodborne illnesses caused by the consumption of contaminated or spoiled food affect 600 million people globally each year (Almli et al., 2022). In common foods, meat products will produce more biogenic amines upon spoilage due to their high protein content (Altmann et al., 2023). Consumption of meat products with high biogenic amine content can lead to diarrhea, headaches, high blood pressure, and other problems (Ruiz-Capillas & Herrero, 2019). Amongst these, pork is one of the most produced and widely distributed meats (Ibeogu et al., 2024). Therefore, monitoring and control of pork is important for resource conservation and human health. Packaging can prolong the shelf life of food and improve its acceptance in the market (Ahmed et al., 2022; Cazón & Vázquez, 2021; Han et al., 2018). Compared to other preservation techniques (dipping, spraying, etc), food packaging can effectively prevent the penetration of preservation materials into the food matrix, which can effectively reduce the impact of these materials on the color, taste and safety of the food (Silva et al., 2023). Nevertheless, traditional food packaging materials play a role mainly through the physical isolation of oxygen and microorganisms, which can only achieve limited fresh-keeping effects (Ding et al., 2022). Furthermore, their extensive use can be harmful to the environment (Laskar & Kumar, 2019). Therefore, smart packaging based on bio-based materials has gained popularity due to its quality monitoring effect, better anti-corrosion properties, and biodegradability (Atta et al., 2022). Common bio-based film materials include polysaccharides, proteins, lipids, and bio-based polyesters (Mohamed et al., 2020; Sid et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2023). Among them, polysaccharides are widely used due to their low cost, wide range of sources, and good biocompatibility (Deng et al., 2022). Chitosan is a linear cationic polysaccharide, which is usually obtained by deacetylating chitin extracted from marine arthropods, insects, or fungi (Mohan et al., 2022). Due to its good film formation, controlled solubility and inherent antimicrobial properties, chitosan has been widely used in food packaging (Florez et al., 2022; Sady et al., 2021). However, chitosan itself has limited bioactivity, which leads to the limited effect provided by chitosan films (Yang, Lan, & Sun, 2023). Therefore, other active ingredients (antimicrobials, antioxidants, color developers, etc.) need to be incorporated into the chitosan films to further enhance their biological activity or confer new properties.

Carbon quantum dots are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with remarkable fluorescent properties (Zhao et al., 2023). Due to their low cost, biocompatibility as well as excellent optical properties, carbon quantum dots have been widely used in medicine imaging (Yang et al., 2023), environment monitoring (Manikandan & Lee, 2022), chemical analysis (Gao et al., 2021), and other fields (Molaei, 2020). Based on the functional characteristics of chitosan and carbon quantum dots, the combination of them has been widely used in smart packaging materials, drug delivery, bio-imaging and other fields (Abazar & Noorbakhsh, 2020; Ostovar et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2022; Zong et al., 2023). Nitrogen-doped citric acid carbon dots (NCD) are one of the most widely used carbon quantum dots, which are commonly obtained by the reaction of amino compounds and citric acid at high temperatures (Strauss et al., 2020). Due to abundant functional groups on the surfaces, optical properties, and biological activity, the incorporation of NCD can effectively improve the mechanical properties, UV shielding ability and biological activity of bio-based films (Lin et al., 2022). Furthermore, the fluorescence of NCD would be enhanced with the increase in pH, which enables it to be used as a fluorescent pH-responsive reagent (Zhang et al., 2020). Compared to traditional pH-responsive reagents (anthocyanins, curcumin, betaine, etc.), NCD has a higher sensitivity. Therefore, NCD can achieve the indicated effect at low addition level. The use of fluorescent indicators is also effective in avoiding interference with the color of foodstuffs (Lin et al., 2022). In addition, the fluorescent properties of NCD with orange emission can be enhanced by surface metal ion functionalization. This enhancement allows for more sensitive biogenic amine monitoring (Qu et al., 2016). Therefore, NCD functionalized with surface metal ions is an ideal material for use as indicators in smart packaging. Notably, the antimicrobial and antioxidant activities of NCD were not significant. Further compounding with antimicrobial components is an effective way to solve this problem.

Ger is an acyclic mono-olefin terpenoid derived from plants. Due to its excellent bioactivity, high biosafety, and unique aroma, Ger has been widely applied in food, cosmetics, and biomedicine (Maczka et al., 2020). However, Ger is poorly water-soluble, volatile and easily oxidized. The formation of inclusion complexes with cyclodextrins is a common method to improve the aqueous solubility and stability of Ger (Kayaci et al., 2014). Nevertheless, unmodified cyclodextrins often do not achieve the desired solubilization effect. The prepared inclusion will not be uniformly dispersed when incorporated into the films, which may have an impact on the mechanical properties and biological activity of the films. HPCD is one of the most commonly used cyclodextrin derivatives. Hydroxypropylation greatly improves the solubility of β-cyclodextrin while retaining the hydrophobic cavity. Inclusion complexes formed by HPCD with functional molecules have been widely used in food packaging materials (Guan et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2024; Li et al., 2022). After compounding Ger with HPCD, the inclusion can be uniformly dispersed in the composite film system and exert the preservative effect through slow release (Aytac et al., 2016). Therefore, the incorporation of NCD with good indication and Ger/HPCD inclusion complex (HPCDGer) with good preservative effect into chitosan film is an effective way to develop new smart packaging.

Herein, NCD with orange emission was synthesized by the reaction of citric acid and urea in a DMF system and its surfaces were functionalized by potassium ions. Inclusion complexes of Ger with HPCD were synthesized by solvent evaporation and freeze-drying methods. Chitosan composite films containing NCD and HPCDGer were prepared by the casting method. The composite film exhibited excellent pH response at 0.5% addition of NCD. Monitoring by fluorescence can prevent the color of the food itself from interfering with the results. In addition, the stronger interaction of NCD with chitosan can avoid its contamination of foodstuffs. HPCDGer has good water solubility, which enables it to be evenly dispersed in the film and exert antimicrobial effect. Furthermore, a range of characterization techniques and tests were employed to analyze the structure, physical and chemical properties of the composite films. Eventually, its application was further evaluated by its preservation effect on pork.

Chitosan (MW = 50 kDa, Deacetylation degree = 85%), HPCD (Degree of hydroxypropylation = 64.07%), and Ger were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid was bought from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Urea was provided from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. 2,2-Di(4-tert-octylphenyl)-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-Azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic Acid Ammonium Salt) (ABTS) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai. China). E. coli (ATCC25922) and S. aureus (ATCC29213) were provided by Jiangnan University. The pork used in the preservation experiments was locally marketed. All other chemicals not mentioned were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, characterization, and pH response of NCD

ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of NCD was obtained by the reported method with some simple modifications (Qu et al., 2016) [Scheme 1 A]. 6 g urea and 3 g anhydrous citric acid were added to 30 mL DMF and sonicated to dissolve them completely. The mixed system was transferred to a Teflon reactor and reacted at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction solution was poured into 60 mL of KOH solution (50 mg/mL) and stirred for 10 min. Then, the mixture was centrifuged (15,000 rpm, 10 min) and collected for precipitation. The resulting precipitate was washed twice using deionized water. Finally, NCD was obtained by vacuum freeze drying.

NCD was characterized by Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis), fluorescence, FT-IR, TGA, XRD, and transmission electron microscope (TEM). The exact test conditions are demonstrated in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of pH-responsive properties of NCD
ce:section.ce:para: The pH response characteristics of NCD were analyzed by color change, fluorescence spectroscopy, and chromaticity coordinate analysis. In brief, NCD was dissolved in deionized water and the pH was adjusted to 2–13, respectively. Afterward, take pictures, measure fluorescence spectra, and calculate optical coordinates.

HPCDGer was prepared in a mixed system of water and acetonitrile (Truzzi et al., 2021). In brief, 2 mmol HPCD and 2 mmol Ger were dissolved in 60 mL of 50% acetonitrile solution. The mixed solution was stirred in a closed container for 24 h. Then, the container was left open in a fume hood overnight to make the acetonitrile evaporate completely. The unencapsulated Ger was removed by liquid separation. Finally, vacuum freeze-drying to obtain HPCDGer solid.

HPCDGer was characterized by TGA, XRD, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The exact test conditions are demonstrated in the Supplementary material. The degree of hydroxypropylation (DH) of HPCD and the encapsulation efficiency (EE) of HPCDGer were calculated by integrating the 1H NMR spectra. The calculation method refers to previous reports (Aytac et al., 2016; Yue et al., 2021). The calculation is shown in equations (1) and (2).

Composite films were prepared using the casting method [Scheme 1B]. Chitosan (1% w/w) was dissolved in a 1% acetic acid solution and stirred until completely dissolved. NCD (0.5 wt%, relative to chitosan) and HPCDGer (50 wt%, relative to chitosan) dissolved in a small amount of water were added to the above solution. After stirring for 1 h, 20 wt% glycerol (relative to chitosan) was added as the plasticizer. Then, the mixture was sonicated to achieve degassing. The film solution (30 g) was poured onto a 10 × 10 cm plastic plate and baked at 37 °C for 48 h. Finally, the films were characterized and analyzed after equilibration at 25 °C, 50% humidity for 24 h. The composite film with NCD and HPCDGer added was named CS/NCD@HPCDGer. The film without the addition of HPCDGer and the chitosan film were named CS/NCD and CS, respectively.

SEM (Hitachi, Japan) was used for structural and thickness measurements on the surface as well as cross-section of composite films. The films were fixed on the conductive adhesive and observed after gold spraying.

The FT-IR spectra of the films were measured using Nicolet IS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet, US). Wavenumber range and resolution are 4000-650 cm−1 and 4 cm−1, respectively. The air was used as a blank control.

XRD was used to analyze the crystalline properties of the films. The films were placed in a sample tray and scanned for XRD spectra in X-ray diffractometer (D2 Phaser, Bruker, Germany). The sweep range and speed are 5–60° and 5°/min respectively.

TGA was used to analyze the mass of the film as a function of temperature. 4–6 mg of film was placed in a 70 μL alumina crucible and measured for TGA curves in a Mettler Toledo TGA2 thermogravimetric analyzer (Zurich, Switzerland). Nitrogen flow rate, ramping rate, and temperature scope are 50 mL/min−1, 20 °C/min−1, and 25–550 °C, respectively.

The color of the composite films was measured using a colorimeter (Ci7600, US). Color features were quantified using lightness (L), redness-greenness (a), yellow-blue (b) and total color difference (ΔE). Among them, ΔE is calculated by the following formula.

Among them, L, a, and b denote the color parameters of the measured film. L*, a*, b* denotes the color parameters of the CS.

Strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB) were used to evaluate the mechanical properties of the composite films. The films were cut into 1 × 4 cm strips and measured in the texture analyzer (TA, TA-XT Plus, Stable Microsystems, UK) for TS and EAB.

The measurement of WVP was performed according to previously reported methods (Li et al., 2022). Anhydrous calcium chloride (3 g) was placed in a 50 mL centrifuge tube and sealed with film. Then, the tubes were placed in an environment with 75% humidity and 25 °C temperature. The centrifuge tubes were weighed every 24 h for 4 consecutive times. The calculation of WVP was based on the following equation.
(4)
WVP =
Δ m × x
Δ P × s
where Δm, x, ΔP, and s stand for mass change after 24 h, thickness of films, effective area, and partial vapor pressure respectively.

The light-blocking ability of the films was evaluated by their UV–vis spectra. The films were cut into 1 × 3 cm strips and placed in a UV 1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) to measure the UV spectrum between 200 and 800 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water solubility, moisture content, and swelling degree
ce:section.ce:para:
The measurements of water solubility, moisture content and swelling degree were made according to the previous method with a simple modification (Zhang, Cao, et al., 2023). The films were cut into 2 × 2 cm squares and weighed (M1). Then, the films were dried under a vacuum at 65 °C for 24 h and weighed again (M2). Dried films were immersed in pure water for 24 h and weighed after excess water was removed using filter paper (M3). Afterward, the films were dried again under vacuum at 65 °C for 24 h and weighed (M4). moisture content, swelling degree, and water solubility were calculated according to the following equation. Each group of experiments was carried out three times in parallel.

The WCA of the films was measured using a contact angle meter (OCA15EC, BOROSA, Germany). First, a drop of water is suspended from the top of the syringe. The films were placed on the loading platform and rose slowly into contact with the droplets. The WCA was measured by goniometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pH response ability of composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The composite films were cut into squares of suitable size and immersed in 5 mL PBS solution. The pH range of PBS solution was 3–12. After 1 h, the color of the composite films under the light of a hand-held ultraviolet lamp was photographed and recorded. The color parameters of films were measured using Adobe Photoshop (Adobe, US).

The antioxidant activity of the composite films was evaluated by the free radical (DPPH and ABTS) scavenging effect. DPPH scavenging activity was determined with reference to the previous method (Wang et al., 2019). DPPH radical was solubilized in ethanol (absorbance at 517 nm is between 0.6 and 0.8 when mixed with an equal volume of water). 50 mg of film was dispersed in 5 mL of water and shaken for 12 h. Then, the two systems were mixed in equal amounts and incubated in the dark for 30 min. DPPH scavenging activity was calculated by measuring the absorbance of the mixed system at 517 nm [Eq. (8)].
(8)
DPPH
scavenging
activity
( % )
=
A 1
−
A 2
A 1
× 100
A1 is the absorbance at 517 nm of DPPH solution mixed with an equal volume of water. A2 represents the absorbance at 517 nm of the DPPH solution mixed with the film solution.

The determination of ABTS scavenging activity was based on previously reported methods with simple modifications (Yang et al., 2016). ABTS solution (7 mM) and potassium persulfate solution (2.45 mM) were mixed and incubated for 12 h at room temperature to obtain ABTS+ solution. 50 mg of film was dispersed in 5 mL of water and shaken for 12 h. Then, 1 mL ABTS+ solution was added to the film solution and incubated under dark conditions for 8 min. Measure the absorbance of the mixture at 734 nm and calculate the removal effect according to the following equation.
(9)

A3 is the absorbance at 734 nm of ABTS + solution mixed with an equal volume of water. A4 represents the absorbance at 734 nm of the ABTS + solution mixed with the film solution.

The modified plate counting method was used to evaluate the antibacterial activity of composite films (Wang, Du, et al., 2019). E. coli and S. aureus are among the most common Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. Due to their close association with foodborne disease, these two bacteria are often used to evaluate the antibacterial properties of antibacterial films (Lu et al., 2016). Therefore, E. coli and S. aureus were used as test bacteria in this section. Single colonies were inoculated in LB liquid medium and cultured at 37 °C and 180 rpm for 6 h. The resulting bacterial solution was diluted to a concentration of 105 CFU/mL for future use.

The ultraviolet-sterilized composite film (60 mg) was placed in a test tube and 5 mL LB liquid medium was added. Then, 5 μL of the above bacterial solution are added to the test tube and cultured at 37 °C and 180 rpm for 3 h. The 100 μL cultured solution was coated in LB solid medium. After cultured at 37 °C for 24 h, the LB plate was photographed and counted. The test tube without the films was used as a blank control. The bacteriostatic rate was calculated by the following formula.
(10)
Inhibition
rate
( % )
=
N c
−
N A
N c
× 100
NC and NA represent the number of colonies in the LB plate of the control group and the experimental group, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preservation and quality monitoring of pork
ce:section.ce:para:
The application of the composite film was further evaluated by its effectiveness in preservation and quality monitoring of pork. Containers containing pork were sealed with films. Then, the pH, weight loss, and total volatile bases nitrogen (TVB-N) of pork as well as color of the films (Under 365 nm excitation) were recorded on day 1, day 3, day 5, and day 7, respectively. Among them, the pH and TVB-N were measured according to the National Standard of the People's Republic of China GB/T 9695.5–2008/ISO 2917: 1999 and GB 5009.228–2016 (2016), respectively. The weight loss was calculated according to the following formula.
(11)
Weight
loss
( % )
=
M t
−
M 0
M 0
× 100
M0 and Mt denote the initial weight and day t weight of the samples, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of NCD in composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The escape of the color developer from the film could contaminate the food. Therefore, the stability of NCD in films was assessed in this section. In brief, the composite films were cut into 2 × 2 cm squares and immersed in 5 mL of water. The fluorescence intensity at 629 nm was measured every 3 days (30 days total). Pure water and NCD solution (with the same NCD content as the film) were used as negative and positive controls, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of the solution soaking the film was used to analyze the stability of NCD in the film.

All experiments were set up with three parallel groups. All data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. *p < 0.05 was used to indicate a significant difference between the data. Statistical analyses were performed by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) using SPSS (IBM, US).

The optical properties, thermal stability, crystalline properties and structure of NCD were passed through UV–vis, fluorescence, TGA, XRD, FT-IR, and TEM. The UV spectrum of NCD is shown in Fig. 1 A. NCD has a UV absorption peak at 535 nm.

The fluorescence spectra of NCD at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 1B. The fluorescence emission peak of NCD is located at 629 nm. The fluorescent intensity increases with increasing excitation wavelength when the excitation wavelength is in the range of 360–540 nm. To avoid the influence of the octave peak and excitation light on the fluorescence intensity of NCD at 629 nm, 520 nm was chosen as the excitation wavelength for the later studies [Fig. 1C].
The TGA and DTG curves of NCD are shown in Fig. S1. NCD has a faster mass loss at 25–120 °C and 350–550 °C, which correspond to water loss and loss of NCD shell respectively (Zhang et al., 2023).

The XRD spectra of citric acid, urea, and NCD are shown in Fig. S2. Citric acid and urea both have multiple sharp peaks between 2θ = 5–60°, which indicates that both have a highly crystalline structure. The high-temperature reaction between the two produces NCD with only two flat peaks at 2θ = 13 and 29°, indicating that NCD has an indeterminate structure.

The FT-IR spectra of citric acid, urea and NCD are illustrated in Fig. 1D. The peaks corresponding to O-H and C=O stretching could be observed in the FT-IR spectrum of citric acid at 3494 and 1709-1754 cm−1 (Chandra & Karak, 2018). For urea, peaks of N-H stretching vibrations, C=O stretching and N-H bending can be observed at 3441, 1678 and 1601 cm−1 respectively. For NCD, the broad peak of NCD at 3409 cm−1 results in the superposition of O-H stretching and N-H stretching vibrations. Furthermore, peaks corresponding to C=O stretching and N-H bending appeared at 1658 and 1588 cm−1. These results suggested that citric acid and urea undergo a cross-linking reaction to produce NCD (Lin et al., 2022).
The TEM image and particle size distribution of NCD are presented in Fig. 1E and F, respectively. NCD is circular in shape with an average particle size of 3.15 ± 0.63 nm.

TGA and DTG curves of HPCD and HPCDGer are shown in Fig. S3. There were significant weight loss processes in HPCD at 40–120 °C and 290–410 °C, which were attributed to water loss and sugar backbone decomposition, respectively. Compared to HPCD, HPCDGer has an additional weight loss between 125 and 275 °C. The weight loss in this range is attributed to Ger detaching from the cavity and volatilizing. According to previous study, free Ger evaporates rapidly between 50 and 220 °C (Kayaci et al., 2014). Therefore, TGA analysis indicated that the thermal stability of Ger was effectively enhanced by the encapsulation of HPCD.

The crystalline property of HPCD and HPCDGer was analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 4 S
, the XRD pattern of HPCD only has two flat diffraction peaks at 12.4 and 18.2°. This indicates that HPCD has an amorphous crystalline structure, which is consistent with previous studies (Shen et al., 2022). The diffraction peaks of HPCDGer and HPCD were not significantly different, indicating that HPCDGer also has an amorphous crystalline structure.

The NMR spectra of HPCD and HPCDGer are presented in Fig. 2. For HPCD, the peaks of H1, H8 and H9 appear at 5.15, 4.19 and 1.07 ppm, respectively. The peak at 3.28–4.05 ppm is formed by the superposition of chemical shifts of H2-7 (Gao et al., 2019; Yue et al., 2017). The DH of HPCD can be calculated as 64.07% by bringing the peak areas of H9 and H2-7 into equation (1). The new peaks appearing in the HPCDGer correspond to chemical shifts of Ger. The peaks at 5.27, 5.02, and 4.10 ppm correspond to the Hb, Hf and Ha of Ger. The peaks of Hd-e and methyl protons of Ger appeared at 1.88–2.05 ppm and 1.48–1.64 ppm respectively (Yue et al., 2017). The EE of Ger can be calculated as 79.01% by substituting the peak area into equation (2).

The microscopic morphology of HPCD and HPCDGer was observed by SEM [Fig. 5S]. The morphology of HPCD is a hollow, spherical particle, which is consistent with previous reports (Wei et al., 2017). After encapsulating Ger, the product showed a lamellar structure with well-defined edges.

The fluorescence colors, fluorescence spectra, and chromaticity coordinates of NCD at different pH are demonstrated in Fig. 3. The fluorescence intensity of NCD in an aqueous solution gradually enhanced with the increase of pH. Their color gradually changed from light orange to orange-red. This change may be attributed to the presence of more carboxylate ions on the NCD surface at high pH. These carboxyl ions result in electron-rich properties on the inner surface of NCD, which can enhance the fluorescence of NCD by increasing its Fermi energy level (Qu et al., 2016). In addition, the chromaticity coordinates of NCD were all between orange and red in the pH range 2–13 [Fig. 3C].

SEM images of the front and cross sections of CS, CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer are shown in Fig. 4. The surfaces of the three kinds of films are smooth and compact, and there are no cracks and bubbles. The thicknesses of CS, CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer are 24.74 ± 0.49, 22.63 ± 0.33, and 36.41 ± 0.92 μm respectively. In addition, the compactness of the microstructure of CS, CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer is gradually increasing, which may be due to the increase of intermolecular forces due to the addition of HPCDGer and NCD.

The FT-IR spectra of CS, CS/NCD, and CS/NCD@HPCDGer are shown in Fig. 5A. For CS and CS/NCD, the peaks at 3313 and 2877 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the O-H and C-H, respectively (Sanandiya et al., 2019). Due to the acetylation, the peaks for C=O stretching (amide Ⅰ), N-H bending (amide Ⅱ) and C-N stretching (amide Ⅲ) appear at 1651, 1577 and 1376 cm−1, respectively (Ilyasoglu et al., 2019). Furthermore, the peaks of axial and angular deformation of C-O bonds as well as C-O-C deformation can be observed at 1156, 1030 and 897 cm−1 (Jiao et al., 2019). Compared to CS and CS/NCD, the absorption peak of CS/NCD@HPCDGer at 2923 cm−1 is stronger, which may be attributed to the -CH3- and -CH2- in HPCD and Ger. Due to the presence of C-O-C in the HPCD, the enhancement of the absorption peak at 1030 cm−1 can also be observed. In addition, the new absorption peaks generated by the introduction of HPCD can be observed at 849, 753 and 706 cm−1 (do Nascimento et al., 2020).

XRD patterns were used to analyze the crystalline properties of the composite films [Fig. 5B]. The XRD patterns of CS, CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer can all be observed with diffraction peaks around 14 and 29°. The diffraction peaks at these two locations are both flat peaks, which suggests that all three films have an amorphous crystalline structure.

The thermodynamic properties of the films were reflected by TGA and DTG curves. TGA curves, DTG curves and weight loss details are shown in Fig. 5C and D and Table S2 respectively. All films showed a significant weight loss at 25–125 °C, which is mainly attributed to the water loss of films. Due to the difference in water content of the films, the weight loss of CS, CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer at this stage is 8.61%, 9.60% and 13.04% respectively. CS and CS/NCD showed weight loss induced by chitosan deacetylation at 125–230 °C. Their rate of weight loss at this stage reached the maximum at around 295 °C. Notably, CS/NCD@HPCDGer has no significant DTG peak at this site, which may be due to the addition of HPCD making the weight loss between 240 and 400 °C more rapid. The weight loss of CS and CS/NCD at 230–400 °C corresponds to the skeleton decomposition of chitosan (Marin-Silva et al., 2023). Deacetylation and decomposition of chitosan as well as decomposition of HPCDGer in CS/NCD@HPCDGer occur at 125–400 °C. Of these, the DTG peaks corresponding to the decomposition of chitosan and HPCDGer were located at 296 and 341.67 °C, respectively (Khan et al., 2023). Finally, the residues of CS, CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer at 550 °C were 33.41%, 34.45% and 25.68% of the initial mass respectively.

The appearance and color values of the films are shown in Fig. 5E and Table 1 , respectively. CS has no discernible color. CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer appear purple and brown, respectively. As shown in Table 1, the addition of NCD makes the film more opaque and improves its redness. Compared to CS/NCD, the incorporation of HPCDGer increased the b value of the film.

The TS and EAB of the films are shown in Table 2 . Due to the limited intermolecular interaction forces, CS has a low TS (10.96 ± 0.88 Mpa). The incorporation of functional molecules can increase the TS by increasing intermolecular interactions. For instance, Lin et al. (2022) prepared chitosan film with a TS of 17.4 Mpa. The addition of carbon points increased the TS of the film to 29.3 Mpa. The study of Cheng et al. (2023) showed that 60% 1,8-cineole/cyclodextrin inclusion complex could increase the TS of PVA/chitosan film from 9.51 Mpa to 11.42 Mpa. The addition of both NCD and HPCDGer significantly increased the TS of the films. The TS for CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer were 14.63 ± 0.64 and 23.44 ± 1.06 Mpa, respectively. The reason for this phenomenon may be the addition of NCD and HPCDGer increased electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding in the composite films (Erceg et al., 2023). There was no significant difference in EAB between CS and CS/NCD, which may be due to the lower addition of NCD. However, the addition of HPCDGer reduced the EAB of the composite film, which is to be attributed to the interaction of HPCDGer with chitosan limiting the movement of chitosan (Yin et al., 2021).

The UV–vis spectra of CS, CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer are shown in Fig. 5F. CS exhibited UV shielding only between 200 and 500 nm. The addition of NCD significantly increased the barrier effect of the composite film on light at 400–800 nm, which is attributed to the high absorbance of NCD in this range. In addition, the addition of HPCDGer further improved the light barrier effect of the composite film. These results indicated that the composite film incorporating NCD and HPCDGer has excellent light barrier capability.

The WVP of the three films is illustrated in Table 2. The WVP of CS is 4.78 ± 0.58 g mm/m2 day kpa. Compared with CS, CS/NCD has a lower WVP (4.33 ± 0.36 g mm/m2 day kpa), which may be due to the abundant functional groups on the surface of NCD that promote cross-linking between chitosan molecules and resulted in a more compact film structure. However, due to the low addition of NCD, the reduction of WVP was not significant. The WVP of CS/NCD@HPCDGer was significantly lower than that of CS and CS/NCD. This phenomenon is to be attributed to the addition of HPCD increasing the hydrogen bonding interactions and thickness of the film (Zhang, Cao, et al., 2023). These changes made it harder for environmental water molecules to cross the film and bind to CaCl2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content, swelling degree and water solubility
ce:section.ce:para: Moisture content, swelling degree, and water solubility of the films are shown in Fig. 6 A–C. As shown in Fig. 6A and B, moisture content and swelling degree of composite films can be improved by the addition of NCD and HPCDGer. This alteration is associated with the hydrophilic nature of NCD and HPCDGer. Among them, HPCD has a greater impact due to its higher addition level. The solubility of CS/NCD@HPCDGer is significantly higher than CS and CS/NCD. This suggests that the addition of highly hydrophilic cyclodextrin inclusion will enhance the solubility of the film in water.

The size and shape of the contact angles of the composite films are shown in Fig. 6D. Due to the characteristic that chitosan can only be dissolved in acidic conditions, CS exhibited a high level of hydrophobicity (WCA = 102.1 ± 1.0°). Due to the hydrophilicity of NCD and HPCDGer, the WCA of CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer were reduced to 92.5 ± 1.5 and 84.3 ± 1.26°, respectively. The change in WCA indicates that the incorporation of the two materials improved the hydrophilicity of the composite film. According to previous reports, films with a WCA greater than 65° are considered to have a hydrophobic surface (Vogler, 1998). This suggests that the composite film incorporating NCD and HPCDGer is still hydrophobic, which indicates its potential application in food packaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH response characteristics of composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence images and color parameters of CS, CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer at different pH are presented in Fig. 7 and Table S3. CS did not show any significant fluorescence at different pH. For CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer, the red-orange fluorescence gradually increases with increasing pH. The pH-responsiveness of the composite films was more pronounced when the pH was higher than 6, which suggests that the composite film can be used to monitor the formation of alkaline environments. The volatile biogenic amines would be produced as the pork spoils. Therefore, these results indicate the potential application of composite films in monitoring the quality of meat products.

The scavenging activities of the three films on DPPH and ABTS radicals are demonstrated in Fig. 8 A and B. The scavenging efficiencies of CS on DPPH and ABTS radicals were 8.71 ± 2.66% and 38.19 ± 1.11%, respectively, which is attributed to the inherent antioxidant activity of chitosan (Soni et al., 2018). NCD and Ger both have excellent antioxidant activity (El Azab et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2018). The scavenging effect of chitosan film incorporating NCD on the two free radicals reached 32.56 ± 2.27% and 71.62 ± 1.51%, respectively. The addition of HPCDGer further enhanced the antioxidant activity of the composite film. The scavenging effect of CS/NCD@HPCDGer on DPPH and ABTS free radicals was 74.01 ± 0.74% and 92.48 ± 0.22%, respectively. These results show that the chitosan film with NCD and HPCDGer has good antioxidant activity, which makes it a potential application prospect in food antioxidation.

The antimicrobial effect and inhibition rate of the composite films are demonstrated in Fig. 8C–E. Due to the antibacterial effect of the amino groups in chitosan, the inhibition rates of CS against E. coli and S. aureus were 11.17 ± 0.46% and 19.64 ± 3.72%, respectively (Harugade et al., 2023). The antibacterial effect of CS can be attributed to the disruptive effect of the amino-positive ion on the biofilm of the bacteria. NCD also has a degree of antibacterial activity (Wang et al., 2022). The inhibition rates of CS/NCD against E. coli and S. aureus were 30.02 ± 1.51% and 54.69 ± 0.92%. The addition of NCD enhanced the antibacterial effect on S. aureus more significantly, which may be due to that NCD exerts its antibacterial effect by interacting with bacterial biofilm. Similar to chitosan, E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria) is more resistant to the antibacterial effects of NCD due to its more complex cell wall structure than S. aureus (Gram-positive bacteria) (Zhao et al., 2020). The addition of HPCDGer resulted in a more significant improvement in the antibacterial effect of the composite film (Inhibition rates for E. coli and S. aureus were 99.82 ± 0.32% and 95.89 ± 0.99%.). In summary, CS/NCD@HPCDGer showed excellent antibacterial activity, which suggested its promising application in the preservation and conservation of foodstuffs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Retain freshness and quality monitoring of pork
ce:section.ce:para: Application prospects of composite films were further evaluated by experiments of retained freshness and quality monitoring. The decomposition of proteins and nitrogenous substances during spoilage of meat products results in an increase in pH, rate of weight loss and TVB-N (Wan et al., 2020). Therefore, pH, weight loss and TVB-N were used to measure pork freshness in composite films [Fig. 9 A–C]. In addition, the pictures of CS, CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer at different times are shown in Fig. S2 (Under 365 nm excitation).

The pH of pork samples not encapsulated by the films increased rapidly with time. The pH of the sample increased from 5.68 ± 0.45 to 8.10 ± 0.17 on day 7, which could be attributed to the accumulation of biogenic amines produced during the spoilage process (Qin et al., 2023). The encapsulation of all three films reduced the rate of increase in pH. Among them, CS/NCD@HPCDGer exhibited the best effect. The pH of the pork samples wrapped in CS/NCD@HPCDGer reached only 6.22 on day 7. This is attributed to the slow volatilization of Ger in CS/NCD@HPCDGer, which effectively inhibited oxidative reactions and microbial propagation in the pork. The production of TVB-N in pork is due to proteolysis caused by microbial fermentation. The TVB-N for fresh pork is 6.23 ± 0.09 mg/100 g. On day 7, TVB-N reached 37.85 ± 1.12, 29.36 ± 0.42, 23.87 ± 1.41, and 10.09 ± 0.40 mg/100 g for control and three films-encapsulated pork, respectively. Only the pork wrapped in parcel CS/NCD@HPCDGer still met the relevant quality standards on day 7, which is also attributed to the antibacterial and antioxidant effects of Ger. Weight loss of pork mainly due to loss of nutrients and water. Relative to the other experimental groups, packaging of CS/NCD@HPCDGer was able to reduce the quality loss of pork samples more significantly. This phenomenon is attributed to the better water-blocking, UV-shielding and biological activity of CS/NCD@HPCDGer, which effectively prevented water loss and nutrient breakdown in pork. In summary, CS/NCD@HPCDGer exhibited excellent food preservation. These results further indicate its promising application in the food packaging field.

The color change of the films (Under 365 nm excitation) during the storage of pork is shown in Fig. S6. Chitosan does not have a quality detection effect. Therefore, the color of CS did not change. CS/NCD showed red fluorescence at day 5, and the red fluorescence was more pronounced at day 7. This change is attributed to the fermentation of microorganisms in the pork, which produced volatile biogenic amines and raised the pH of the film (Gu et al., 2023). CS/NCD@HPCDGer was observed to be distinctly red fluorescent only on day 7. The color difference between CS/NCD and CS/NCD@HPCDGer is to be attributed to the slow release of Ger in HPCDGer, which effectively inhibited microbial fermentation and reduced biogenic amine production. These results indicate that composite film can effectively monitor the quality of pork and delay spoilage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Escape characteristics of NCD in composite film
ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan, HPCD and Ger in CS/NCD@HPCDGer have been recognized as safe and widely used in food systems. Although NCD was considered safe in some research, it is not known whether NCD in film can penetrate into food matrices with water molecules in the environment and affect the color, taste as well as the safety of the food (Han et al., 2021). Therefore, the safety of CS/NCD@HPCDGer was further evaluated by testing whether NCD escapes in the film. The test results are shown in Fig. 9D. Even after 30 days of immersion in pure water, there was still no visible fluorescence in the immersion solution. This suggested that the NCD in composite film had high stability and would not escape from the film. Notably, NCD is a water-soluble material. The ability of NCD to remain stable in thin films may be attributed to the abundance of functional groups on its surface, which allowed for a stronger interaction with chitosan. In summary, the test results demonstrate the safety of CS/NCD@HPCDGer and further confirm its potential for food applications.

In conclusion, this study prepared chitosan film containing NCD and HPCDGer by casting method. The incorporation of NCD and HPCDGer improves the TS, hydrophilicity, UV shielding ability, water-blocking effect, antioxidant activity and antimicrobial activity of chitosan film. NCD confers on the film an orange fluorescence enhanced with increasing pH. In the pork preservation experiment, the pork samples wrapped in CS/NCD@HPCDGer remained fresh for 7 days. The freshness retention effect of the composite film is attributed to its antimicrobial and antioxidant activity as well as UV shielding ability, which efficiently prevents the oxidation and decomposition of nutrients. The composite film can also respond to amine vapor from pork spoilage to achieve the indication effect. In addition, the NCD in the film will not be affected by water molecule that spills out and contaminates the food matrix. These results suggest that the composite film has a promising application in the preservation and quality control of pork. Noteworthy, this study confirmed the ability of the composite film to retain freshness and monitor biogenic amines through a freshness preservation experiment. In subsequent studies, the relationship between volatile amine concentration and film fluorescence can be explored to establish a more accurate response relationship.

Xiaowei Ding: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xuerong Luo: Writing – review & editing. Wenting Shi: Writing – review & editing. Dekun Cheng: Writing – review & editing. Xiang He: Resources. Lin Yue: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zhouping Wang: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.
